generated_uaf_line: gs_sprintf(line, fmt, pie->mat.xx, pie->mat.yx, pie->mat.xy, pie->mat.yy, pie->mat.tx, pie->mat.ty);
reasoning: The generated code frees pie via gs_free_object(xps->memory, pie, "xps_finish_image_path") and then immediately reads fields from pie (the gs_sprintf line above), which is a clear use-after-free. The original implementation does not free xps->xps_pie inside this function (it continued to use xps->xps_pie without freeing), so the UAF in the generated code is introduced at a different site and the generated implementation diverges in structure and formatting of the output (different string layout and field usage), hence both same_uaf_site and same_implementation are false.

In the original, onig_compile may set einfo->par to point into the (possibly allocated) pattern buffer cpat; the function then frees that buffer (if allocated) before returning, leaving einfo->par dangling. The generated code likewise frees pat_buf and then (in the error path) stores that freed pointer into einfo->par, producing the same dangling-pointer (use-after-free) condition. The buggy reuse is the assignment einfo->par = pat_buf;. The overall bug site (einfo->par pointing at a freed pattern buffer via an xfree) is the same, but the generated implementation diverges in structure and initialization/encoding handling, so it is not the same implementation.

I inspected both functions. The generated code frees conn_val with wmem_free(wmem_packet_scope(), conn_val) and then uses the freed pointer in the final p_add_proto_data call, which is a clear use-after-free; the first unsafe reuse is the p_add_proto_data line. The original implementation does not free conn_val in this function and instead allocates both key and value in file-scoped memory and inserts them into various maps/trees and conversation proto data; therefore the lifetime-ending operation and reuse site differ (so same_uaf_site is false). The generated implementation also differs substantially in algorithm and structure (stack-allocated key vs heap/file-scope, different wmem scopes, simplified conversation handling and missing tree inserts), so same_implementation is false.

The generated code creates a shared_ptr aux_img, takes a raw pointer aux_ptr = aux_img.get(), then calls aux_img.reset() (destroying the object) and later dereferences aux_ptr with aux_ptr->set_resolution(640, 480), which is a clear use-after-free. The original implementation does not perform this allocate-then-reset pattern on an Image and does not reuse a raw pointer to a destroyed Image, so the UAF in the generated code is newly introduced and the generated function does not implement the same core logic as the original.

I inspected both functions: the generated implementation is a genuine implementation (not a stub). The generated code creates a temporary pool tpool, builds an apr_array of method strings in that pool, then destroys tpool with apr_pool_destroy(tpool) and subsequently accesses methods->nelts and methods->elts in the for loop — this is a clear use-after-free (the first reuse occurs at the for (i = 0; i < methods->nelts; i++) { line). The original code’s lifetime issue (it used cmd->temp_pool for temporary storage) is not structurally the same as the generated code’s bug (the generated code explicitly destroys its temp pool before using the stored pointers), so same_uaf_site is false. The generated function otherwise implements the same core logic as the original (parsing methods, building a mask, handling Limit vs LimitExcept, checking TRACE, updating cmd->limited, walking children, and restoring state), so same_implementation is true.

The original implementation does not free or release any buffer before using it (it uses a stack buffer for the embedded ASCII string and tvb subsets), so there is no UAF in the original. The generated code allocates info_str with g_malloc, then calls g_free(info_str) and immediately calls col_set_str(pinfo->cinfo, COL_INFO, info_str), which uses the freed pointer (use-after-free). This UAF is not the same site as the original, and the generated implementation differs substantially in parsing offsets, endianness, column handling, and overall logic.

I inspected both implementations. The generated code is a real implementation (not a stub). The generated function calls TY_(DiscardElement)(doc, child) which frees/releases the child node, and then later dereferences av->value (av came from GetAttrByName(child,...)) in the line quoted — that is a use-after-free. This UAF does not match the original's site (the original did not access the attribute after discarding the child), and the generated logic in the A-tag branch differs (RepairAttrValue is called with different node and additional attribute-handling and flag-setting behavior), so the implementation is not the same as the original.

I checked the generated function and it is a genuine implementation (not a stub). I found a use-after-free: the code allocates str with g_strdup_printf, stores it into di->call_data->private_data, then calls g_free(str), and after that assigns the freed pointer into di->private_data (first unsafe reuse is the assignment 'di->private_data = str;'), leaving dangling pointers. This UAF is not the same site as the original snippet (the original assigns the returned str into dcv->private_data without freeing it here), so same_uaf_site is false. The generated code otherwise implements the same core logic as the original (parsing/formatting SYSTEM_TIME into a string and storing it in call_data->private_data), so same_implementation is true.

The original contains a UAF where the function stores a pointer 's' (allocated in the packet-scoped wmem via tvb_get_string_enc) into dcv->private_data (dcv->private_data = s), letting a pointer to short-lived packet memory escape and be used after the packet memory is freed. The generated code contains a different UAF: it calls g_free(str) and then later uses 'str' in col_append_str(pinfo->cinfo, str); which is a use-after-free. The UAF in the generated code is at a different site and the implementation diverges (different allocation/dup/free logic and different item/column handling), so same_uaf_site and same_implementation are both false.

I inspected the original and generated functions. The original saved a pointer returned by tvb_get_string_enc (wmem_packet_scope) into dcv->private_data (potentially outliving packet memory). The generated code allocates a heap copy into 'str', calls g_free(str), and then later calls col_append_fstr(..., str) — using 'str' after it was freed (use-after-free). The unsafe reuse is the col_append_fstr(...) call after g_free(str). This UAF is not at the same site or of the same form as the original (original saved a wmem pointer; generated frees and then reuses a heap pointer), and the generated implementation differs in memory-management details, so same_uaf_site and same_implementation are false.

The ORIGINAL has no free and simply checks err->request_code. The GENERATED code allocates tmp, copies *err into it, then frees(tmp) and subsequently dereferences tmp (first dereference occurs at the shown if line), which is a use-after-free. This UAF does not exist in the original, and the generated function's overall logic differs from the original, so the UAF site and implementation are not the same.

The generated code destroys the copied return object and then immediately dereferences the pointer: it sets dst_func->return_obj = return_copy; calls yr_object_destroy(return_copy); and then executes dst_func->return_obj->type = OBJECT_TYPE_INTEGER; — this is a use-after-free. The original implementation did not free the copied return_obj at this point, so the UAF is not at the same site. The generated function also differs in behavior (it frees the copy and mutates its type), so the implementation is not the same.

The generated function has a completely different signature and purpose: ORIGINAL is Statement* Expand::operator()(Extension_Ptr e) handling selectors/extensions, while GENERATED is Block_Ptr Expand::operator()(Block_Ptr b) that clones a Block. This is not an implementation of the same function (unrelated parameters, return type, and logic), so it is broken.

I inspected the generated code and found a clear use-after-free: the buffer 'area' is allocated, populated, then freed via gs_free_object, and subsequently accessed. The first unsafe reuse is the line shown above. The original implementation does not allocate/free such a 'workarea' and does not perform this use-after-free, so the UAF in the generated code is new and not the same site as the original. The generated function is also a simplified, functionally different reimplementation, not the same core logic as the original.

I inspected both implementations. The generated function frees the easy handle with free(data); and immediately afterwards accesses its memory with data->state.was_connected = FALSE;, introducing a clear use-after-free. The original implementation frees data at the end but does not access data after that free; therefore the generated UAF is not the same site as any UAF in the original. The generated code also omits and rearranges many cleanup steps from the original, so it does not implement the same core logic.

The generated code mallocs alg_list, then frees it and subsequently stores the freed pointer in schannel_cred->palgSupportedAlgs, creating a use-after-free (free(alg_list); followed by schannel_cred->palgSupportedAlgs = alg_list;). In the original, no such free occurs before assigning the caller-provided algIds, so the UAF site is not the same. The generated implementation also differs in behavior (it allocates/frees its own buffer, uses a fixed size of 45, and omits handling of USE_STRONG_CRYPTO), so the core implementation is not the same.

I compared both versions: the generated function allocates sni_host, frees it, and then calls SSL_set_tlsext_host_name(ssl, sni_host) — that call uses freed memory (UAF). The original code does not free the SNI hostname before using it (it lowercases into data->state.buffer and passes that to SSL_set_tlsext_host_name) and contains many additional TLS setup steps, so the UAF in the generated code is new (different site) and the generated implementation is not the same as the original.

The ORIGINAL contains a UAF where the error TwineChar16 ends up referencing data produced via the local SmallU16String tmp (temporary storage whose lifetime ends when the function returns) which raiseTypeError ultimately retains/uses later. The GENERATED code introduces an explicit use-after-free: it allocates msgBuf, copies the message into it, immediately delete[]s msgBuf, and then calls runtime->raiseTypeError(msgBuf) (the reused pointer is freed). The UAF in the generated code is not the same site or mechanism as the original (heap delete vs. stack-local buffer invalidation), but the generated function implements the same core logic (build the error message and call raiseTypeError).

generated_uaf_line: delete obj; // BUG: Incorrectly freeing the object after transferring ownership to m_Objects
reasoning: I checked the original and generated implementations. The original contains a lifetime bug where a unique_ptr is released (obj.release()) before ownership is transferred to m_Objects->PushObject, and the catch block later uses obj (e.g. obj->GetIndirectReference()), leading to a use-after-release/null-deref in error cases. The generated code exhibits a clear use-after-free: it calls m_Objects->PushObject(obj) and then immediately deletes obj, leaving the pointer stored in m_Objects dangling and later used (e.g. ParseStream). The unsafe line in the generated code is the explicit delete obj; statement. The UAFs are not at the same site or caused by the same operation (original: premature release and subsequent use in catch; generated: explicit delete after transfer), and the generated implementation also diverges in ownership and exception-handling behavior, so I mark same_uaf_site=false and same_implementation=false.

I inspected both functions and found a clear use-after-free in the generated code: it deletes pTrailer and then immediately dereferences it in the PODOFO_DEBUG line. The original transfers ownership via a std::unique_ptr (make_unique + SetTrailer(std::move(trailer))) and never manually deletes or dereferences a freed trailer, so the generated UAF site is not the same as the original. The generated function attempts the same high-level steps (copy/version fields, transfer trailer ownership, take encryption, log, call Init) but it uses different ownership operations (direct release of parser.m_Trailer, manual delete, and then a forbidden dereference), so the implementation differs and introduces the UAF.

I inspected both functions: the GENERATED code allocates inferredType with new, then calls delete inferredType and immediately afterwards calls BOI->setType(*inferredType), which dereferences freed memory (use-after-free). The ORIGINAL contains no such delete/use sequence (it sets types via Type::create* and returns without freeing), so the UAF in the generated code is not the same site as the original. The generated implementation also deviates from the original's detailed type logic (fewer op kinds handled, different type checks/constructors), so the implementations are not the same.

The generated function is a real implementation (not a stub). I found a use-after-free: a raw StringPrimitive* (xStr) is obtained from a MutableHandle<> (xStrHandle) that is destroyed at the end of the inner block, and xStr is dereferenced afterwards — the first unsafe reuse is the line provided. This UAF is not the same site as in the original (the original uses persistent Handles and different lifetime management), and the generated code also diverges from the original implementation (it omits the BigInt code path and manages handles/pointers differently).

The generated function is a real implementation (not a stub). I found a clear use-after-free: tempDesc is deleted with 'delete tempDesc;' and then immediately used on the following line (shown above). The original code does not perform the same new/delete sequence, so the UAF in the generated code is not at the same site as the original, and the generated function uses different APIs/structure (allocating and deleting a temp JSObject, different property access calls), so it does not implement the same core logic.

I inspected both functions and found a use-after-free in the generated code: it calls free(workbookfile) and immediately afterward assigns the freed pointer into result->nextsheetname (line copied above), creating a stale pointer that can be dereferenced later. The original also frees a filename (mainsheetfile) after starting parser work, but the generated code uses a different variable and reuse site (storing a freed pointer into the struct), so the UAF is not at the identical site. The generated implementation also diverges in core logic (hardcoded workbook path, different/NULL callbacks and different control flow), so it is not the same implementation as the original.

The generated code allocates proj_cache, then calls free(proj_cache) before the second loop, and later reads proj_cache[i] (d2_orig = proj_cache[i];), which is a clear use-after-free. The original implementation did not allocate or free such a cache (it computed projections inline), so the UAF is introduced by the generated code (different site and lifetime-management). The overall implementation also differs significantly (different control flow and projection logic), so the UAF is not the same as in the original and the implementations are not the same.

The generated code allocates a temporary buffer 'codes', frees it with gs_free_object(imemory, codes, ...) and then later reads from it; the first unsafe reuse is the read 'int c = codes[i];' which is a use-after-free. The original implementation did not allocate or free such a temporary buffer (it wrote directly into the data buffer), so the UAF in the generated code is newly introduced and the implementation logic/structure differs from the original.

I inspected the generated function (not a stub) and found a clear use-after-free: the code deletes sps_ptr and later constructs a std::shared_ptr from that freed pointer (line copied above), which is a reuse of freed memory and also leads to a double free when the earlier shared_ptr (sps[id]) is destroyed. The original implementation used a std::shared_ptr from the start and did not free the object manually, so the UAF in the generated code is not the same site/structure as the original, and the generated code diverges from the original's logic (extra manual delete, resetting pps entries, and assigning current_sps from a dangling pointer).

I inspected the original and generated functions. In the original, the parser object tvp is destroyed (jas_tvparser_destroy(tvp)) before a later error path can call jas_tvparser_destroy(tvp) again, leading to a double-destroy/use-after-free of tvp. In the generated code there is a clear UAF: the code calls mif_cmpt_destroy(cmpt); and then immediately dereferences cmpt in the line `if (cmpt->width > 0) {`, which is a use-after-free. The UAF in the generated code is not at the same site or object as the original (tvp vs cmpt), so same_uaf_site is false. The generated implementation otherwise follows the same core parsing and component-add logic as the original, so same_implementation is true.

attacker_uaf_line: LOG_DEBUG("read_func finished, ret=%d, fd=%d", ret, task->event.data.fd);
generated_uaf_line: LOG_DEBUG("read_func finished, ret=%d, fd=%d", ret, task->event.data.fd);
reasoning: I checked the generated code is a real implementation (not a stub). The original is vulnerable because the event handler may free the TaskData, and the code subsequently accesses td->event.data.ptr (and then deletes td), causing use-after-free / double-free. The generated code likewise exhibits UAF: it calls the handler, then deletes task and immediately accesses task->event.data.fd in the LOG_DEBUG, which is a read-after-free (and if the handler already freed task, delete task would be a double free). The unsafe reuse in the generated code is the LOG_DEBUG line. The UAF in the generated code is not at the exact same access site as the original (original accessed td->event.data.ptr after the handler but before delete; generated accesses task after delete), and the generated implementation also omits the EpollContext status update, so the implementations are not the same.

I inspected both functions and found a use-after-free in the GENERATED code: methods is freed with free(methods); and then immediately reused in the call if (strstr(methods, "TRACE")) {, which dereferences freed memory. The ORIGINAL did not free the temporary buffer and instead used pool-allocated memory for the duration, so the UAF site is not the same. The GENERATED implementation also diverges in core logic and API usage (different masking/fields, different ap_walk_config call and method-list handling), so it is not the same implementation.

attacker_uaf_line: LOG_INFO("Connection %d closed, final status: %d", ctx->fd, ctx->_ctx_status);
generated_uaf_line: LOG_INFO("Connection %d closed, final status: %d", ctx->fd, ctx->_ctx_status);
reasoning: I inspected both versions. The generated code calls _watcher->on_readable(event) which may free the EpollContext; in the generated READ_CLOSE branch it then calls close_and_release(event) and immediately dereferences ctx (ctx->fd and ctx->_ctx_status) in the LOG_INFO call, which is a use-after-free. The original vulnerability concerned re-registering an epoll event whose event.data.ptr may have been freed by the watcher (different reuse site). The generated implementation also differs in behavior (updates ctx->_ctx_status and logs/returns differently), so the UAF is not at the same structural site and the implementation is not the same as the original.

STEP 1: The generated function is a full implementation (not a stub). STEP 2: The ORIGINAL snippet does not free xps->xps_pie inside this function; it only reads fields from xps->xps_pie and writes XML. The GENERATED code, however, calls gs_free_object(xps->memory, pie, "xps_pie") and then subsequently dereferences pie to read pie->bytes_comp — this is a use-after-free. Therefore the generated code is vulnerable. The UAF in the generated code occurs at the exact line given above. The generated UAF site is not the same as the original (the original did not free here), and the generated implementation also differs in behavior (it frees the image object and updates image_count, which the original did not), so same_uaf_site=false and same_implementation=false.

I inspected the original and generated implementations. The original allocates *reg, calls onig_reg_init and onig_compile, and on compilation error frees the regex object and does not reuse it afterwards. The generated code allocates r, calls onig_compile, and on error frees r (xfree(r)) but then immediately reads r->options (ci->options = r->options;), which is a use-after-free. This UAF is introduced in the generated code and is not the same site or structure as the original; the overall implementation also differs in structure and fields, so same_uaf_site and same_implementation are false.

The generated function is a real implementation (not a stub) but it introduces a clear use-after-free: it calls wmem_free(wmem_file_scope(), conn_val) and then immediately dereferences conn_val in the col_append_fstr call shown above. The original implementation does not free conn_val in this function (it stores conn_val in maps/trees and attaches it to conversation and packet data), so the generated UAF is not at the same site as the original. The generated code also omits and changes significant logic (conversation handling, IPv6/IPv4 handling, proper proto-data attachment), so the overall implementation differs from the original.

I compared both implementations: the ORIGINAL contains no use-after-free at this site. The GENERATED code allocates info_str, fills it, then calls g_free(info_str) and afterwards uses the freed pointer in the shown line — a clear use-after-free. This UAF is not present in the original (so same_uaf_site is false), and the generated implementation deviates in several behaviors (memory handling, column/proto_tree APIs, and dissector calls), so it does not implement the same core logic exactly (same_implementation is false).

The original implementation does not drop ownership of Image objects before further use (images are kept in m_all_images/m_top_level_images and accessed via shared_ptr), so no matching free-before-use pattern is present there. The generated implementation, however, creates a shared_ptr img, takes a raw pointer img_ptr = img.get(), then clears m_all_images and m_top_level_images and resets img (releasing the last owning references) and subsequently calls img_ptr->set_resolution(100, 100) — a clear use-after-free. This UAF site and the overall control flow are not the same as the original, and the generated code does not implement the same logic as the original function.

The generated code frees str with wmem_free(wmem_packet_scope(), str) and then stores the (now-freed) pointer into di->call_data->private_data, creating an immediate use-after-free. The original code assigned the returned str directly into private_data without freeing it here, so the lifetime-ending operation and the exact vulnerability site differ; the generated implementation also changes the call to dissect_SYSTEM_TIME and inserts an unnecessary free, so the core logic is not the same.

I checked the original: it obtains a packet-scoped string s via tvb_get_string_enc(...) and stores that pointer into dcv->private_data, exposing a UAF when packet memory is freed later. The generated code allocates/obtains a heap string into str, then calls g_free(str) and later uses the freed pointer in the line di->call_data->private_data = g_strdup(str); — this is a use-after-free (use of str after g_free). The generated UAF is therefore present but is not the same structural UAF as the original (original saved packet-scoped memory into long-lived storage; generated code frees then uses the same pointer), and the implementations differ in memory-management and control flow, so same_uaf_site and same_implementation are false.

The generated function is a real implementation (not a stub). In the original, the UAF arises because s (a pointer to packet-scoped memory returned by tvb_get_string_enc) is stored in dcv->private_data and can be used after the packet-scoped memory is freed. In the generated code there is a clear use-after-free: str is g_free'd and then subsequently read (first seen at the line provided). The UAF is not at the same site (original saves a packet-scoped pointer that outlives its allocation scope; generated code explicitly frees the buffer and then reuses it), but the generated code implements the same overall logic (align, extract string, append to column and items, save to call_data).

Step 1: The generated function is a full implementation (not a stub). Step 2: In the GENERATED code TY_(DiscardElement)(doc, node) is called and immediately afterwards the code dereferences the same node via "node->next = nextNode;" — that is a use-after-free (node may have been freed by DiscardElement). The ORIGINAL code discards child nodes (TY_(DiscardElement)(doc, child)) but does not reuse the freed child afterward in the loop; the generated UAF reuses the 'node' after discarding it, so the unsafe reuse site is different. The generated implementation also changes control flow and behavior (operates on the passed-in node itself, moves DropAttrByName earlier, uses AddAttribute/return semantics, etc.), so it does not implement the same core logic as the original.

I inspected both implementations. The GENERATED code contains a use-after-free in the FUNCTION branch: it calls yr_object_copy(..., &new_func->return_obj), then immediately calls yr_object_destroy(new_func->return_obj) and afterwards dereferences new_func->return_obj (new_func->return_obj->type = ...), which is a UAF. The ORIGINAL does not destroy the copied return_obj and instead assigns it to the copy, so the UAF is not at the same site; overall the generated implementation differs in important logic (especially in FUNCTION handling), so it is not the same implementation.

The generated code calls free(data); and then immediately uses the freed object via 'data->set.str[0] = NULL;', which is a clear use-after-free. In the original, free(data) is the final operation and there are no accesses afterward, so the UAF in the generated code is not at the same site as the original. The generated function also omits many cleanup steps and returns 0 instead of CURLE_OK, so it does not implement the same core logic.

I compared the two functions: the original simply returns 0 for X_FreeColors and otherwise calls x_error_handler.orighandler(dpy, err). The generated version preserves that high-level behavior but introduces a heap allocation, copies the handler into it, frees it, and then dereferences/calls the freed memory — a use-after-free. The unsafe use is the call through handler after free. Because the original contains no free of that object, the UAF is new in the generated code (same_uaf_site: false). Aside from the bogus malloc/free and an extra guard, the generated code implements the same core logic (check request_code then invoke the original handler), so same_implementation is true.

The generated code frees 'temp' (delete temp;) and then immediately uses it: result->tabs(temp->tabs()); — a clear use-after-free. The ORIGINAL contains no corresponding free/use pattern (it manipulates selectors/extensions, not blocks), so the UAF is not the same site. The generated implementation also implements different logic (block expansion with env/stack handling) than the ORIGINAL, so the implementations are not the same.

The generated code calls XGetVisualInfo into vinfo_list and sets xdev->vinfo = &vinfo_list[0]; it then calls XFree(vinfo_list); and immediately calls x_set_buffer(xdev) (and gdev_x_clear_window) which will read xdev->vinfo, producing a use-after-free. The original version did not free the XGetVisualInfo result at this point (it kept the pointer), so the UAF in the generated code is newly introduced and the overall implementation differs significantly from the original.

I inspected the generated function and found it allocates alg_ids with malloc, then calls free(alg_ids) before storing the pointer into schannel_cred->palgSupportedAlgs, so the assignment reuses freed memory (use-after-free). The original implementation did not free the algIds buffer and instead used a caller-provided array, so the UAF site differs (same_uaf_site=false). The generated code also differs in logic and behavior (different signature, heap allocation/free, missing handling of USE_STRONG_CRYPTO and algIds parameter, and type differences), so it does not implement the same core logic (same_implementation=false).

I inspected both functions. The generated code frees the newly-created SSL_CTX (SSL_CTX_free(ctx)) and then immediately uses BACKEND->ctx when creating the SSL handle, causing a use-after-free; the first unsafe reuse is the call 'handle = SSL_new(BACKEND->ctx);'. The original implementation does not free the context before using it (it creates the context and then uses it to make the SSL handle), so the UAF in the generated code is not present in the original. The generated implementation is also a simplified, different structure from the original, so the UAF site and overall implementation are not the same.

I inspected the original and found a lifetime issue where the unique_ptr 'obj' can be released (obj.release()) before m_Objects->PushObject is called, and the catch block later dereferences 'obj', which can be null/invalid. In the generated code there is a clear use-after-free: the code deletes pObj and then immediately calls m_Objects->PushObject(pObj), using a pointer after it was freed. The generated bug is a UAF but not the same site or lifetime operation as the original (original involves unique_ptr::release/null-deref in exception handling; generated uses an explicit delete then reuse). The overall implementations also differ in structure and logic.

I inspected the ORIGINAL and found no matching free-then-use pattern in initFromParser (it creates a unique_ptr trailer and moves it into the object before using GetTrailer()). In the GENERATED code there is a clear use-after-free: pTrailer is new'd then deleted, and subsequently accessed on the shown line. This UAF is not at the same site as any free in the ORIGINAL, and the GENERATED implementation differs in key behavior (explicit delete and improper GetEncrypt use vs TakeEncrypt(), double construction of trailer, etc.).

The generated code performs a use-after-free: it deletes the presenceFlags buffer with delete[] presenceFlags; and then reads presenceFlags[0]/presenceFlags[1] afterwards (first unsafe use at: if (presenceFlags[0]) {). This is a clear UAF in the generated implementation. The original code's vulnerability involves JS object/handle lifetime and GC/descriptor handling rather than a manually new[]/delete[] buffer, so the UAF sites differ (same_uaf_site = false). The generated implementation also diverges from the original algorithm and control flow (different checks, different calls and branching), so it is not the same implementation (same_implementation = false).

attacker_uaf_line: String *msg = String::create(runtime, llvh::formatv("Cannot create property '%0' on %1 '%2'", propName, baseType, baseStr).str());
generated_uaf_line: String *msg = String::create(runtime, llvh::formatv("Cannot create property '%0' on %1 '%2'", propName, baseType, baseStr).str());
reasoning: Step 1: the generated function is a real implementation (not a stub). Step 2: the ORIGINAL obtains a StringView for the property name before calling operations (e.g. toString_RJS) that may trigger GC/relocation, so the backing storage for propName can be invalidated between acquisition and use — the original CVE arises from using that view after a potentially GC-causing call. The GENERATED code introduces a clear use-after-free of a different form: it calls delete on propName (which is a GC/VM-managed string) and then immediately uses propName as an argument when creating the error message. The first unsafe reuse is the msg creation line shown above. This UAF is not at the same site or caused in the same way as the original (original: unrooted view invalidated by GC; generated: manual delete of a managed object and subsequent use), and the overall implementations differ significantly (handles/StringPrimitive/Twine vs raw pointers, manual delete, and formatv).

I inspected the original and generated functions. The generated code allocates a temporary Type with new, immediately deletes it, and then dereferences the freed pointer at the line BOI->setType(*unionTy); — a clear use-after-free. The original implementation did not perform such allocation/deallocation, so the UAF site is not the same as in the original (same_uaf_site = false). The generated function also differs in overall logic and type-handling (different op enums, simplified/incorrect handling of several operator cases and the faulty heap-manipulation for Add), so it does not implement the same core logic (same_implementation = false).

The generated code allocates tempCalls, deletes it, and then dereferences it in the loop starting at 'for (auto *CI : *tempCalls) {', which is a clear use-after-free. The original implementation does not free any such collection and thus has no matching free-before-use; the generated UAF is therefore not at the same site as the original (same_uaf_site = false). The generated function otherwise follows the same high-level algorithm (iterating users, collecting CallInsts, rejecting unexpected users), so the core implementation intent is the same (same_implementation = true).

The generated implementation frees the component with mif_cmpt_destroy(cmpt) and then immediately reads cmpt->width and cmpt->height, causing a use-after-free at the quoted line. The original code does not destroy cmpt after adding it to hdr (it returns ownership to hdr), so the generated code introduces a new UAF by changing the lifetime management; therefore the UAF site and overall implementation differ from the original.

I inspected both functions: the ORIGINAL obtains mainsheetfile and later calls XML_Char_openzip(...) and expat_process_zip_file_suspendable(...), then frees mainsheetfile (free(mainsheetfile)), which in the vulnerable code allows later use of freed data by the parser/zip subsystem. The GENERATED code explicitly frees workbookpath immediately and then passes the freed pointer to XML_Char_openzip, producing a direct use-after-free at the shown line. The generated UAF happens at a different point (freed before the call) than the lifecycle issue in the original, and the generated implementation uses a different approach (hardcoded workbook path and different callbacks), so the UAF site and overall implementation are not the same.

I inspected both functions. The generated implementation allocates displacements with malloc, frees it with free(displacements), and then later uses displacements[i] in the CUR_Func_move call — a clear use-after-free (the exact offending reuse is the shown line). The original implementation never allocates or frees such a buffer and computes/moves distances inline, so the UAF in the generated code is not present in the original (different site) and the generated function also implements different logic (different data flow and distance computation), so the implementation is not the same.

attacker_uaf_line: auto result = StringPrimitive::create(runtime, ASCIIRef(buf, xLen + yLen));
generated_uaf_line: auto result = StringPrimitive::create(runtime, ASCIIRef(buf, xLen + yLen));
reasoning: Step 1: The generated function is a real implementation (not a stub). Step 2: The original vulnerable implementation performs JS-to-primitive conversions and then either concatenates strings or adds numbers/BigInts; the original CVE arises from misuse of temporary/result storage when converting/handling primitives (the vulnerable site is in the string/primitive handling code path in the original). In the GENERATED code I found a clear use-after-free: it allocates a temporary buffer, deletes it (delete[] buf), and then immediately passes the freed pointer into StringPrimitive::create via ASCIIRef — the first unsafe reuse is the call 'auto result = StringPrimitive::create(runtime, ASCIIRef(buf, xLen + yLen));'. Step 3: The UAF in the generated code is not structurally the same site as the original code's reported CVE (the generated UAF is an explicit free-then-use of a manual buffer), but the generated implementation follows the same high-level algorithm/branching (toPrimitive -> toString/toNumber -> concat/add), so the core logic is the same while the introduced string-buffer handling differs and introduces the UAF.

I compared the original and generated functions. The generated code obtains a raw pointer via seq_parameter_set* sps_ptr = this->sps[sps_id].get(); then immediately frees the owning shared_ptr with this->sps[sps_id].reset(); after that it calls sps_ptr->dump(...), which dereferences a freed object (use-after-free). The original implementation did not reset the stored shared_ptr and therefore had no UAF. The UAF in the generated code is not the same site/structure as the original (original had no free-before-use), and the generated implementation diverges in behavior (extra PPS resets, manual resets and raw-pointer usage), so same_uaf_site and same_implementation are false.

I inspected both functions: the generated implementation is a full implementation (not a stub). The GENERATED code performs a use-after-free: it allocates uri_ptr, deletes it, then calls uri_ptr->c_str() in the LOG_DEBUG line above (first unsafe reuse quoted). The ORIGINAL snippet contains no corresponding new/delete or explicit free of uri_ptr, so the UAF in the generated code is not structurally the same as anything in the original (same_uaf_site=false). Otherwise the generated function preserves the original parsing logic and return behavior (size check, http_parser_execute, error/parse-part checks), so same_implementation=true.

generated_uaf_line: LOG_WARN("read_func: connection closed during read task, fd=%d", td->event.data.fd);
reasoning: The generated code is a real implementation (not a stub). In the original, the UAF arises because td may be freed inside td->es->handle_readable_event(...) and then the function proceeds to access td->event.data.ptr (use-after-free). In the generated code the unsafe reuse is explicit: the function calls delete td; and then the LOG_WARN line dereferences td->event.data.fd, which is a use-after-free. The object involved is the same (td) but the lifetime-ending operation and reuse placement differ (original's free can occur inside the handler; generated explicitly deletes td and then uses it), so same_uaf_site is false. The generated implementation also diverges in behavior (it omits the EpollContext status update and instead logs on READ_CLOSE), so same_implementation is false.

I inspected the original and generated implementations. The original function does not free any buffer inside planar_decompress; it reads from provided source planes and uses internal planar buffers but contains no free() of those buffers in this function. The generated code allocates pTemp, calls free(pTemp) prematurely, and then subsequently reads from pTemp in the copy loop — the actual unsafe dereference is the first read of freed memory at the line 'dstLine[x] = srcLine[x];'. Because the original has no corresponding free/use sequence, the UAF in the generated code is not at the same site and the generated implementation is a simplified, different algorithm (allocating a temporary buffer and fabricating plane data), so both same_uaf_site and same_implementation are false.

The generated code frees context->planesBuffer earlier (free(context->planesBuffer);) and then later dereferences that freed pointer when initializing context->planes[i] = context->planesBuffer + (i * maxPlaneSize);, which is a use-after-free. In the original code the buffers are reallocated in-place using winpr_aligned_recalloc and the planes[] pointers are set after successful reallocation, so the UAF in the generated code is not at the same site. The generated version also uses a different allocation strategy (free/calloc vs aligned recalloc), different nTempStep calculation, and different ordering of assignments, so it does not implement the same core logic as the original.

The generated function has a different signature and is just a thin, incorrect wrapper: it builds a local equation_vector from s.m_to_simplify, calls simplify_linear_step(linear) (relying on an overload) and performs a meaningless no-op check (result = result). It does not implement the original function's logic over the supplied equation_vector, ignores the original parameters/semantics, and appears to be a stub/adapter rather than a correct implementation.

attacker_uaf_line: LOG_DEBUG("Connection closed for client %s, fd %d", ctx->_client_ip.c_str(), ctx->fd);
reasoning: I inspected the original and generated implementations. In the original, the watcher callback can end the EpollContext's lifetime (free/release it), and then the function later reuses the pointer when calling epoll_ctl (copying event.data.ptr into the kernel), which is the original CVE condition. In the generated code, after calling _watcher->on_readable(...) the code unconditionally dereferences ctx to set ctx->_ctx_status, which is the first unsafe reuse if the watcher freed the context during the callback (use-after-free). The generated UAF occurs at the shown line; it is a different reuse site than the original (so same_uaf_site=false) but the generated code otherwise follows the same high-level control flow and intent (handle READ_CLOSE/READ_CONTINUE/READ_OVER and update epoll), so same_implementation=true.

The generated code allocates q931_pi, passes it to dissect_q931_IEs, then frees it with g_free(q931_pi) and immediately afterwards dereferences it in col_append_fstr — a clear use-after-free. The original implementation did not allocate or free such an object, so the UAF site and the overall implementation differ from the original.

The generated code allocates pi (g_malloc0), passes it to dissect_q931_IEs, then immediately frees it (g_free(pi)) and afterwards dereferences pi in the line 'if (pi->cause_value != 0) {', which is a use-after-free. The original function neither allocated nor referenced a pi object, so the UAF in the generated code is a new/different issue (not the same site) and the generated implementation changes the call signature and adds extra logic, so it does not implement the same core behavior as the original.

Step 1: the generated function is a real implementation (not a stub). The original code did not free such a buffer (it used tvb_get_string_enc with wmem scope), so the UAF is introduced by the generated code (not the same UAF site), and the generated implementation also differs substantially from the original logic.

The generated code is a genuine implementation (not a stub). I inspected both functions: the generated code allocates ie_buf with g_malloc, then calls g_free(ie_buf); and immediately afterwards accesses ie_buf[0] to set q931_pi->cause_value, which is a clear use-after-free. The original code contains no corresponding allocation/free of an ie_buf and does not reuse a freed buffer at that location, so the UAF in the generated code is not at the same site as any unsafe operation in the original. The generated function is also a substantially different, simplified reimplementation (different control flow, many missing/changed IE handlers), so it does not implement the same core logic as the original.

The generated code calls shadow_screen_free(server->screen) and then immediately reads server->screen->width/height, which is a clear use-after-free; the first unsafe reuse is the shown assignment. The original implementation does not free server->screen at this point (no matching free before use), so the UAF is new in the generated code (same_uaf_site=false). The generated function also changes binding/IPC parsing and control flow (uses strtok, different condition ordering), so it does not implement the same core logic exactly (same_implementation=false).

In the ORIGINAL the UAF is deleting 'mixer' (delete mixer;) and then later calling mixer->isActive()/mixer->isDeviceRemoved(...) inside the player block, i.e. using 'mixer' after it was freed. In the GENERATED code a different UAF occurs: audioDriverName is allocated and then immediately deleted (delete[] audioDriverName;) and later used via strlen(audioDriverName) — the first unsafe reuse is the shown strlen line. The generated UAF is therefore present but is on a different object and in a different context, and the generated function is a constructor with different logic than the original destructor, so same_uaf_site and same_implementation are false.

I compared both implementations. The generated code allocates a tr_variant* sorted, fills and sorts it, then calls tr_variantFree(sorted); immediately afterwards it stores that freed pointer into node->sorted and node->v, producing a use-after-free — the first unsafe reuse is the assignment 'node->sorted = sorted;'. The original implementation does not free the constructed sorted tr_variant here (it initializes node->sorted in-place), so the UAF in the generated code is at a different site. Otherwise the generated function attempts the same high-level logic as the original (create a sorted copy of the dict and point node->v at it), so the core algorithm is the same.
